title: What's a Reasonable Timeline for Web Design Projects?
excerpt: Setting realistic timeline expectations before a web design project begins prevents frustration and protects relationships. This post explains what a typical web design timeline looks like, what each phase involves, and what you can do to keep things moving.
content:
One of the most common sources of frustration in web design projects is misaligned timeline expectations. Clients want fast. Designers need thoroughness. Here's what a professional web design timeline actually looks like — and how to plan around it.

Typical Web Design Timeline Phases

Phase 1: Discovery and Strategy (1–2 weeks) Includes a kickoff meeting, brief review, competitor research, and goal setting. This phase produces a clear project brief and site map that everyone agrees on. Phase 2: Wireframing (1–2 weeks) Low-fidelity layouts showing content structure and navigation. No visual design yet — just layout logic. Client review and approval required. Phase 3: Visual Design (2–4 weeks) Applying the visual identity (colors, typography, imagery) to the wireframes to create high-fidelity mockups. This is the stage clients most often envision. Multiple rounds of review. Phase 4: Development (2–6 weeks) Building the designs in code — front-end development, CMS setup, and back-end integration where required. Timeline depends heavily on complexity. Phase 5: Content Integration (1–2 weeks) Populating the site with real content — copy, images, video. Often delayed by client-side content delivery. Phase 6: Testing and QA (1 week) Browser and device testing, performance optimization, bug fixing, and final approval. Phase 7: Launch (1–3 days) DNS migration, final checks, and going live.

Total Timelines at a Glance

Project TypeTypical Total Timeline
Simple site (3–5 pages)4–6 weeks
Standard business site (5–15 pages)6–10 weeks
E-commerce or complex site10–20 weeks

What Causes Delays?

The most common causes of timeline overrun are on the client side: slow feedback, late content delivery, and mid-project scope changes. Clear processes and responsive communication from both sides keep projects on track.

We Respect Your Timeline
